summary: I grew up under the reign of Nicolae Ceaușescu, the communist leader of Romania whose power lasted from 1965 until 1989. His regime was oppressive, and even minor criticisms were dangerous. My father, a journalist, became a critic of the government and after publishing a piece about the government's downfalls, he disappeared. Living under Ceaușescu's rule meant constant fear of persecution and the secret police.

I was born and raised in Romania, specifically in Bucharest, during a time when the country was under strict communist rule. Life there was dangerous, especially for those who spoke out against the government like my father. After his disappearance, my family had to flee Romania to escape persecution. Romania was my home, but the fear and oppression made it impossible to stay.

Bucharest was where I spent my early years, growing up in the bustling capital of Romania. Both my parents worked there—my father as a journalist and my mother as well. It was a city filled with tension under Ceaușescu's regime, and the fear of government crackdowns was always present. Leaving Bucharest behind was heartbreaking but necessary for our safety.

After my father disappeared, my family fled Romania and took refuge in a camp in Ukraine. The conditions there were harsh—dirty and grimy—but it was the only place we had at the time. We stayed in the refugee camp for a while before deciding to start anew by moving to the United States. Ukraine was a difficult but necessary stop on our journey to freedom.

My family and I came to the United States as refugees in 1982 after leaving Ukraine. We arrived in Phoenix, Arizona, hoping to start over and find safety. The U.S. was a place of freedom and opportunity, very different from the fear we experienced in Romania. It took time to adjust, but eventually, I felt safe, accepted, and at home here.

Arizona became our new home when we arrived in the United States, chosen because my mother had distant family here. The heat was a shock to me, so different from what I was used to in Romania and Ukraine. Living in Arizona meant adapting to a new culture, language, and climate, but it was where I began to feel safe and hopeful for the future.

We settled in Chandler, Arizona, where my mother's distant relatives lived. This community helped us feel less alone as we adjusted to life in America. It was here that I started school, made friends, and even took up baseball, which helped me fit in better. Chandler became the place where I finally felt at home after all the hardships we endured.
